coredata.dc:title: Nonlinear optical properties and spectroscopic analysis of NB-pyrrole isosteres: Theoretical investigation
coredata.dc:creator: Gad, Shaimaa F.
El Kelany, Khaled E.
El-Mehasseb, Ibrahim M.
El-Demerdash, Safinaz H.
coredata.dc:description: The nonlinear optical properties and spectroscopic analysis of mono and double NB- substituted pyrrole, namely: PN3B2 - PN3B4 - PN2B3 - PN2B4 - PN3,4N2,5, and PN2,5B3,4 have been investigated using two levels of theory: MP2/6-311+G (2df,2p) and DFT B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p). The vibrational spectra spam the range of 400 – 4000 cm−1, where each structure possesses 9 stretching, 8 bending, and 7 torsions modes, with only CH vibrational modes appeared in the mono-substituted group. The energy gap of the mono-substituted group is generally lower than that the double-substituted one. The most stable PN3B2 and PN3,4B2,5 isosteres show the maximum excitation energy and blueshift wavelengths. In solution, the bands are redshifted. The largest chemical shifts for the PN3B4 isostere's NH1 and BH1 protons are found at 8.5 and 4.8 ppm, respectively. The polarizability improves linearly as a function of the dipole moment. The PN2B4 introduces the highest dipole moment and consequently the polarizability, while PN3B2 shows opposite trend.

Gaussian 09 calculations for FT-IR spectra of NB substituted pyrrole analogues at B3LYP /6-311++G(d,p).
Gaussian 09 calculations for FT-Raman spectra of NB substituted pyrrole analogues at B3LYP /6-311++G(d,p)
Simulated UV/Vis absorption spectra for pyrrole, its mono and double substituted NB isosteres at TD-B3LYP/6-311 ++ G**.

NTOs for the excitation with significant and small but non-negligible oscillation strengths for the studied structures at the B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) level with solvent effects of DMSO through SMD. The displayed occupied (holes) and unoccupied (particles) NTO pairs are only that have contribution more than 50% to each excited state (eV).

The calculated FT-IR, FT-Raman, wavenumbers (cm−1) and Raman intensity (IRa) using B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) along with the assignments of the fundamental vibrations and total energy distribution for NB pyrrole isosteres. Values of MP2/6-311+G (2df, 2p) are in parentheses.

head.ce:title: Nonlinear optical properties and spectroscopic analysis of NB-pyrrole isosteres: Theoretical investigation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nonlinear optical properties and spectroscopic analysis of mono and double NB- substituted pyrrole, namely: PN3B2 - PN3B4 - PN2B3 - PN2B4 - PN3,4N2,5, and PN2,5B3,4 have been investigated using two levels of theory: MP2/6-311+G (2df,2p) and DFT B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p). The vibrational spectra spam the range of 400 – 4000 cm−1, where each structure possesses 9 stretching, 8 bending, and 7 torsions modes, with only CH vibrational modes appeared in the mono-substituted group. The energy gap of the mono-substituted group is generally lower than that the double-substituted one. The most stable PN3B2 and PN3,4B2,5 isosteres show the maximum excitation energy and blueshift wavelengths. In solution, the bands are redshifted. The largest chemical shifts for the PN3B4 isostere's NH1 and BH1 protons are found at 8.5 and 4.8 ppm, respectively. The polarizability improves linearly as a function of the dipole moment. The PN2B4 introduces the highest dipole moment and consequently the polarizability, while PN3B2 shows opposite trend.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceuticals, anti-oxidants, and dyestuff are just a few of the various uses for heterocycles, which contain one or more heteroatoms [1]. Since more than 90 years ago, several researchers (including Dewar, Ashe III, Liu, and others) have studied the notion of isoelectronic structures, as well as the substitution of the CC pair with the isoelectronic N-B unit, to synthesize and build novel heterocyclic compounds [2]. Concerns are being raised concerning the possibility of an isoelectronic NB unit replacing one or more CC units in a molecule [3,4]. Because of the polarity of the NB pair [5,6] such structures have unique electronic and optical properties [7] that can be used in a range of sectors, including organic electronics [8], hydrogen storage [9], synthetic chemistry [10], and biomedicine [11].

Nonlinear optical (NLO) materials that generate extremely efficient second-harmonic blue-violet light have numerous uses, including optical disc data storage, information processing, color display, medical diagnostics, and laser remote sensing [12,13]. In addition, (NLO) materials make excellent photoelectrochemical [14] and fluorescence sensing [15] probes. Significantly, the HOMO-LUMO gap can be changed by selective NB-substitution [16], affecting molecular absorption and emission properties. Boron-containing materials have gotten a lot of attention since this heteroatom is known to reduce the HOMO-LUMO gap while also enabling a range of ways to enhance physical properties through interaction with different Lewis bases [17]. Organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) [18], organic photovoltaics (OPVs) [19], and organic field-effect transistors (OFETs) [20] are all examples of organic devices.

The Huckel-aromatic cyclopentadiene anion's CC component was subsequently substituted with an NB moiety, resulting in a rich and versatile chemistry for the isoelectronic 1,2-azaborolyl rings [21]. A variety of molecular and electrical structures of 1,3diaza,2boroles were explored, synthesized, and thoroughly evaluated [22]. Del Bene et al. previously investigated the structures, bonding relative energies, and aromaticity of borazine, diazadiborines, and pseudoborazine using computational methods [23]. Isomers with N-B-N-B frames were found to be more stable than those with NN or B-B bonds. Liew et al. [24] synthesised five-membered Boratriazaroles and compared their photophysical and electrical characteristics to those of carbon equivalent triazoles. L. Weber [21] investigated the synthesis and reactivity of 1,3diaza,2boroles 1.

The current research can be considered as a continuation for our previous publication [25] which reported the stability and the geometrical properties of the considered NB-pyrrole isosteres. Here, we examine explicitly the further spectroscopic properties of the abovementioned NB-substituted pyrroles including NMR, FTIR, Raman, UV–Visible via applying the DFT-B3LYP and MP2 approaches in gas phase and solvation medium of DMSO and chloroform. Furthermore, the impact of nitrogen and boron doping on the NLO characteristics for the suggested five membered azaborole heterocycles is introduced and highlighted. We also investigate the influence of how the quantity and distribution of NB dopants will affect the optoelectronic properties of NB-doped pyrrole.

To optimize the quantum chemical computations, the Gaussian 09 [26] and GaussView 5.0.9 [27] software packages were employed. To comprehensively investigate the nonlinear optical characteristics and spectroscopic analysis of all NB isosteres of pyrrole, the density functional theory B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) [28,29] and single point calculation ab initio MP2/6-311+G (2df, 2p) [30,31] were utilized.

The vibrational wavenumbers of the NB-pyrrole analogues were calculated using the same level of optimization and the basis stated under the phrase freq = Raman in order to identify them as a minimum or transition state on the potential energy surface. The ChemCraft 1.6 [32] program was used to visualize Gaussian output, where the vibrational calculations performed on the optimized geometries confirm the absence of any imaginary frequencies and emphasize the true minima structures on PES (potential energy surface). Veda4 program [33] was used to perform the animation features and to provide the assignments of the vibrational modes. Total energy distribution (TED) calculations were performed using the SQM method [34,35] using the output files generated following the frequency calculations. We can theoretically describe each mode using TED calculations, which show the proportionate contributions of the redundant internal coordinates to each regular vibrational mode of the molecule.

Electronic absorption spectra for the investigated NB structures were discovered using quantum chemistry techniques TD-DFT (TD-BELYP/6-311++G(d,p)) [29,28], which were utilized in conjunction with SMD continuum solvation models [36] to analyze UV–visible in gas phase and two solvent (DMSO and chloroform). This spectrum was modelled using the Gauss-Sum programme [37]. The natural transition orbitals (NTOs) [38] were studied for each electron excitation rather than only the canonical orbitals. Chemcraft was used to create the NTOs. The NB isosteres of pyrrole analogues were studied using nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (NMR). The gauge-independent atomic orbital (GIAO) approach was used to determine NMR shielding [39–41]. Then, using the equivalent TMS shielding determined before at the same theoretical levels as a reference in vivo, relative chemical changes were projected in vacuum, DMSO, and chloroform.

Linear and nonlinear optical properties such as dipole moment (μ), average polarizability (α), anisotropy polarizability (Δα), and first order hyperpolarizability (β) of our studied structures were calculated using equations in literature [42–45]. Thus, the first order (χ (1)), and second order (χ (2)) electric susceptibility tensors static nonlinear responses were investigated using the finite-field method [46,47], and can be defined as follow.

The considered system is previously reported in our recent work [25], where six distinct NB-dopped pyrrole isosteres were investigated. These isosteres were divided into two classes. The first group is an NB mono substitution (PN3B2 - PN3B4 - PN2B3- PN2B4), whereas the second group is an NB multi substitution (PN3,4B2,5 - PN2,5B3,4). In work [25] all structural, geometrical, and energetic properties of the considered systems are reported. Thermodynamic stability calculations at all stages in this theoretical inquiry show that the PN3B2 form is the most stable isostere of the first group in the gas phase, while the PN3,4B2,5 form is the most stable isostere of the second type [25].

In Fig. 1 , the optimized structures are displayed. To prevent repetition in the labelling of different isosteres, the numbering system is shown in Fig. 1. For example, in the first group, PN2B3 denotes that boron lies at position 3 and nitrogen at position 2, while PN3B2 denotes that nitrogen located at position 3, and so on. In contrast, in the second group, the example PN3,4B2,5 means that the two B atoms are located at positions 2 and 5 while the two nitrogen atoms are positioned at 3 and 4 sites.

In order to explore the structure and stability of inorganic isosteres, infrared (FT-IR) and Raman (FT-Raman) spectra were used [52]. The NB-substituted pyrrole analogues with 10 atoms have a non-linear isosteric structure that results in 24 normal modes of vibration. There were no imaginary frequencies observed in the IR and Raman vibrational spectra, as shown in Figs. 2 and 3
. The vibrations of the CH, CC, CN, N-B, NH, and B-H bands create the most noticeable absorption bands. This indicates that boron and nitrogen atoms are crucial for structural representation. Total energy distribution (TED) values for each vibrational mode, relative frequency, and assignment are gathered in Table 1 .

In fact, B3LYP is a fast, precise, and computationally efficient method for determining the vibrational frequencies of a chemical [53,54]. For example, the stretching vibration of CH of pyrrole is obtained at 3231 (3284), 3242 (3295), 3259 (3308), 3264 (3315) and 3673 (3698) cm−1 using B3LYP and (MP2) and compared to the experimental value (3131.5) [55]. In the Raman and IR spectra, these peaks are less intense, although the CH bending has noticeable beaks. The CC/CN vibrational stretching bands, according to B3LYP data, run from 1065 to 1449 cm−1. When electron correlation is partially included in DFT, the B3LYP wavenumber values are lowered in comparison to the MP2 wavenumber values.

The mass and electrical properties, mesomere or inductive [56] of the substituents, affect where the CH or CC vibration bands are in the range of (3000–3100) cm−1 in the infrared spectrum, some aromatic compositions exhibit four or three peaks as a result of the stretching interaction vibrations of CH bonds [57]. These peaks are restricted to the first group isosteres (PN2B3 - PN3B2 - PN3B4 - PN2B4) in the current study.

In contrast to their extreme weakness in IR spectra, the stretching vibration of CH peaks may be clearly seen in Raman spectra at the same wavenumbers. The PN3B2 isostere, exhibits CH stretching at 3243 and 3265 cm−1. The CH vibrational stretching of other isosteres may be seen at 3199, and 3270 cm−1 for PN2B3 and PN3B4 isosteres, respectively and for PN2B4 isosteres, the modes are 3227, 3207, 3208, 3245 cm−1. These modes are pure stretching ones as expected, and confirmed as shown by the TED column (nearly 100% TED), Table 1.

In this study, the CH in-plane bending vibrations were assigned to the medium intense bands observed at the range of 986 to 1475 cm−1; however, the out-of-plane (CH) mode is obtained in the range of 846–1617 cm−1. These Raman active vibrational modes are less intense at the same wavenumber. See Figs. 2, 3.

The general area of CC stretching vibration in aromatic compounds [58] are observed in the range 1625 – 1430 cm−1. This vibration modes appear only in PN2B3 and PN3B2 isosteres. The symmetric stretching vibrations of the latter isostere (PN3B2) are recognized at 1547, 1617 cm−1 with 95% and 52 % TED values, respectively. On the other hand, the asymmetric vibrational stretch appears at 1121 and 1401 cm−1 with 22% and 15%TED values, respectively. These bands are appeared as characteristically medium and less intense peaks in IR and Raman spectra. The isostere (PN3B2) show bending in plane HCC at 1140 and 1409 cm−1 with 71 and 59 % TED percentage, respectively, while the out-plane bending HCC is shown at 1068 cm−1 and 1224 cm−1 with 16% and 22%. See Table 1.

The identification of CN stretching vibration is a very hard assignment since, the mixing of bands is probable in this region. Peaks in the CN stretching vibrations can be seen in the 350–1000 cm−1 high area [55,59], while the CN stretching vibrations of our systems is larger than that values and detected at medium and less intense peaks for IR and Raman, respectively.

These peaks are restricted to the first group (PN2B3 - PN3B2 - PN3B4 - PN2B4). For PN3B2, the stretching mode was observed in IR and Raman spectra at 1068, 1113, 1366, and 1401 cm−1, while for PN2B3, the band was observed at 1042 cm−1 with 76 %TED and this mode appears at 1050, 1153, 1198, 1406, 1563 cm−1 for PN3B4. Finally, the CN vibrational stretching of PN2B4 isostere, is appeared at 1069, 1077, 1243, 1475, and 1521 cm−1. On conclusion, the highest wave number of vibrational modes is belonging to PN2B4. The in-plane C
N bending indicated at the range from 778 to 1198 cm−1. Out-of-plane C
N bending vibrations are detected from 1176 to 1563 cm−1.

The frequencies around 2600 cm −1 and 3600 cm −1 with 100% TED correspond to B–H and NH stretching, respectively. This indicates that the NH is strong chemical bond and has a higher force constant due to higher frequency. We conclude that, the hydrogen atoms in the B–H bond are more acidic than that in the NH bond. This distinction reveals how acidic and basic hydrogen atoms are. The average relative hydrogen acidities are similar to the six inorganic isosteres. In their IR and Raman spectra, the peaks reflect clearly the number of, e.g., B–H bonds, which are one and two in mono and double-NB-pyrrole, respectively, while NH bonds have two and three peaks.

For NB stretching vibrations (first group of N-B pyrrole isosteres), the IR and Raman spectra display medium absorbance in the range of 922 – 1400 cm −1 which is assignment to the N-B bond in the ring. This vibrational mode appears only for the three isosteres PN2B3, PN3B2, and PN3B4 at different frequencies, see Table 1.

For the second group, The N-B stretching vibrations have less and high intense in Raman and IR spectra respectively, in the range of 890–1470 cm−1. For PN3,4B2,5 isostere, the symmetric stretching vibrations are seen at 1020 cm−1 with 35% while the asymmetric stretching vibrations are observed at 1139 and 1378 cm−1 with 47% and 52%, respectively. For PN2,5B3,4 isostere, the symmetric stretching vibrations is appeared at 1120 cm−1 and 1266 with 34% and 45%, while the asymmetric stretching is observed at 1470 cm−1 with 16 %. The in-plane N-B bending indicated at the range from 707 to 1500 cm−1, while out-of-plane N-B bending vibrations are noticed at 766 to 1330 cm−1.

Finally, the vibrational spectra (FTIR and FT-Raman) in terms of vibrational assignments and relative intensities were obtained in the 4000–400 cm−1 regions. There are 9 stretching, 8 bending, and 7 torsions modes in each group. The mono substituted group has fewer numbers of recognized NH, BH, N B, and NN vibrational modes than the double substituted group since the latter one contains two boron and three nitrogen atoms. Only mono-substituted groups exhibit CH vibrational modes.

Table 1 gives the relevant changes observed in the assignments for our series of compounds. We discuss some significant changes for instance, in the second group, the in plane bending βip BNN and βip NNN are obtained at 766 and 776 cm−1, with a blue shift of 10 cm−1 when B is replaced by N, respectively. On the other hand, in mono substituted groups, other assignments are appeared at the same wavenumbers. For example, the out plane bending βop BNC (75%) vibrational mode of and the in plane bending βip BCN (71%) vibrational mode appear at 798 and 778 cm−1, respectively. Furthermore, the first group includes vibrational torsions such as HNBN (77%) at 533 cm−1, HCCN (85%) at 697 cm−1, and HBNC (77%) TED at 832 cm−1, while the torsion of double substituted group BNNN is shown at 391 and 401 cm−1.

The molecular electrostatic potential (MEP), which is connected to the molecule's overall charge distribution, reveals correlations between molecular features such as dipole moments, partial charges, chemical reactivity, and electronegativity. To forecast the electron rich and poor centers, the MEP of the structures under investigation was calculated using the B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) technique in our previous work [25]. Our previous results demonstrate that a negative electrostatic potential zone exists surrounding the nitrogen and carbon atoms. The hydrogen and boron atoms have a maximal positive area, indicating a likely target for the nucleophilic assault.

One of the most often used methods in pharmaceutical analysis is UV–Visible spectrophotometry [60]. Knowing the absorption bands is crucial since it is possible to quantify the therapeutic material using the wavelength of maximum absorption (λmax). This spectrum also demonstrates the frontier orbital transitions that could result in atoms coming together to form bonds. The transition configurations of the intense peaks, as well as their maximum absorption wavelength (λmax), excitation energy (Eex), oscillator strength (f), and other parameters are collected in Table 2 using TD-DFT calculation. The ab initio methods demonstrate that adding more polarization functions has little effect on excitation energies. Additionally, adding diffuse functions lengthens computation times [61] therefore all calculations were performed using the DFT (B3LYP).

All NB-pyrrole analogues have three distinctive peaks in the UV area and absorb energy in a region with low wavelengths, often below 320 nm. The NB-pyrrole has a substantial development in its absorption spectra that starts between (140 and 160 nm). The second faint peak at (190–240 nm), followed by a third beak appears at (240–300 nm. See Fig. 4 . We discover that all isosteres of pyrrole exhibit short bathochromic (red shift) wavelengths that extend to 350 nm. According to Fig. 4, In the first group, the PN2B4 and PN3B4 isosteres have the highest red shifts (extended to 340 and 400 nm) respectively, whereas the PN3B2 and PN2B3 isosteres have the lowest red shifts (extended to 260 nm). The spectra of the second group included the 120–250 nm wavelength regions. The PN3,4B2,5, and PN2,5B3,4 isosteres both show lower red shift So, we used DFT data for discussion.

The maximum wavelength in the electronic absorption spectra of NB pyrrole relative to parent are bathochromic by 60 and 40 nm, as would be expected given the lower Eg values of isostere PN2B4 and PN3B4 respectively. (See our data of energy gap for previous studies [25]. Mono-NB pyrrole analogues exhibit high electronic absorption, which is due to HOMO to LUMO+1 transitions (97% for the PN2B4 isomer). The most stable isostere, PN3B2 has 92% for HUMO-1 to LUMO+4 transition and has a maximum absorption peak at a shorter wavelength than PN3B4, which is due to transitions from HOMO to LUMO+1 (88%). See Table 2. For the majority of the multi-NB unit's strong electronic absorption, the PN3,4B2,5 isomers, which is related from HOMO-1 to LUMO+4 and HOMO to LUMO+8 transitions. According to Table 2, each of the investigated isosteres contributed differently to the substantial electrical absorption.

The UV–Visible spectrum study of our NB-pyrrole isosteres in chloroform and DMSO was explored also for theoretical calculation. Electronic excitation energy (E), oscillator strengths (f), and absorption wavelengths (λ) are computed and compared to the gas phase calculations. The computed time dependent density functional theory TD-DFT/6-311++G (d,p) approach was used. The calculated absorption maxima values increase from gas phase, then chloroform to DMSO showing bathochromic shift in all isosteres except PN3B2, PN3B4 and PN2,5B3,4 isosteres. These three isosteres show Hypochromic shift (See Table 2). They also show that the solvents DMSO, and, to a lesser extent, CHCl3 increase electronic excitation energy, oscillator strengths and energy gap when compared to the gas phase.

In order to analyze the type of absorption, Fig. 5 shows the NTOs for the highly excited states of the systems under consideration. The terms “hole” and “particles” transition orbitals are used to refer to the occupied and empty NTOs, respectively. In comparison to canonical orbitals, NTOs typically provide a simpler description of the excited state. The organic system (pyrrole) belongs to the C2v point group. The inorganic structures fall under the C1 point group, with the exception of PN3,4B2,5, which belongs to C2 point group and also PN3B4 and PN3B2 isomers fall under Cs. Every electronic excitation is mentioned are π → π* transition, with some assistance from n − π* excitation. This slows down the analysis of excitations. The particles NTOs are primarily delocalized over NH and CH groups, the hole NTOs of all structures under study are delocalized over the entire molecular skeleton.

Nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (NMR) is really used to illustrate the structure of organic molecules. Given that the NB-pyrrole isosteres stated above are novel compounds, it would be beneficial for experimentalists to provide theoretical 13C and 1H. The results are shown in Tables 3 and 4
of gas phase and solvent, respectively using the gauge-including atomic orbital (GIAO) technique in regard to the 13C and 1H isotropic chemical shielding of Si (CH3)4 at the B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) level of theory, in vacuum as well as in two different solvents; DMSO and CHCl3. Our results specific B3LYP are good agreement with NMR spectra of pyrrole [62]. For 1H NMR chemical shift, the hydrogen atoms bound to the carbon atoms in organic compounds range from 6 to 8 ppm [63]. According to our research, the chemical shift values for NB isosteres range from 4.47 to 8.51 ppm in 1H NMR for the first group. Both the NH1 proton and the BH1 proton undergo important chemical modifications, a downfield shift and an up-field proton signal, respectively.

In the first group, the NH1 proton was assigned at highest chemical shifts (8.51 ppm) for the PN3B4 isostere. This is brought on by the positive charge on the nitrogen atom (electron-poor region). Additionally, due to large electron density of the B atoms, the BH1 proton signal of PN2B4 isostere has the lowest chemical shift (4.47 ppm). While the CH1 proton related to the carbon atoms (C3 and C5) that is positioned at the downfield chemical shift is 5.9 and 6.2 for PN2B3 and PN3B4, respectively). The CH1 proton with an up-field signal is associated to the PN2B4 isostere at 7.9 ppm. In the second group isosteres, the NH1 proton (strong downfield shift) and BH1 proton (strong up-field shift) have proton signal of 4.89 and 7.59 ppm for PN2,5B3,4 – PN3,4B2,5 structures, respectively.

The estimated 13C NMR chemical shifts for mono - NB substituted isomers are in the range of 114.39–149.91 ppm. The highest chemical shift is found for C5 of PN2B3 structure (149.95 ppm) (down field). There is a sizable field shift as a result of the C5′s connection to the N atom's high electronegativity and the sp2 CC groups. The electronic distributions in the connection between the nitrogen atom and the nearby carbon atom are polarized because the nitrogen atom has a relatively high electronegativity. Similarly, the C4 of PN2B3 has a 114.39 ppm low chemical shift (up field).

Moreover, the calculated 1H NMR chemical shifts in two solvents reveal an agreement, to some extent, with those reported (experimentally and theoretically) by Katritzky et. al. [64]. for pyrrole (with maximum deviation of about 1.5 ppm). Actually, the NB substitution causes an increase in the chemical shift of carbons (downfield) comparing to other shifts in the parent ring. The electron distributions in the N atom's link with a neighboring carbon atom are polarized as a result of the N atom's comparatively high electronegativity, which raises the chemical shift of carbon atoms. Finally, since NB-PYRROLE is a new compound, its 13C and 1H NMR chemical shifts have been calculated in vacuum and solvents to assist in its preparation and identification.

First hyperpolarizability (β), polarizability (α), and dipolar moment (μ) are three essential metrics for the study of molecular materials' nonlinear optical properties (NLO). Polarizability, which is critical in determining a system's structural, orientational, dynamic, and thermodynamic features, reveals the distribution of electrons within a molecule. The average polarizability (α), anisotropy of polarizability (Δα), dipole moment (μ), first order hyperpolarizability (β), the hyper-Rayleigh scattering (βHRS), and depolarization ratio (DR) of the investigated NB-isosteres of pyrrole are among the linear and nonlinear optical properties listed in Tables 5 and 6
using MP2 and B3LYP methods, respectively in the static mode. The estimated values have been transformed into electrostatic units (e.s.u.) (for α; 1 a.u = 0.1482 × 10–24 e.s.u., for β; 1 a.u = 8.6393 × 10–33 e.s.u.).

All isosteres' predicted dipole moment (µ) values were discovered to fall between very wide ranges from 0.812 to 4.838 D at MP2. In comparison to predictions made by MP2/6-311G (2df, 2p) calculations, dipole moments as estimated by B3LYP/6-311+G (d,p) calculations are less. These results imply that the selected basis set affects the values of the estimated dipole moments. The bigger value of the electronegativity difference is shown by the non-zero dipole moment of the NB pyrrole isosteres. The mono-NB substituted (PN3B2) and multi-NB isosteres PN3,4B2,5 of the first and second types, respectively have the lowest values of dipole moment (0.812 and 0.998 D, respectively). Every NB isostere, with the exception of PN3B2 and PN3,4B2,5 has a higher polarizability than their hydrocarbon equivalents (pyrrole). The PN2B4 isostere has the highest polarizability value and has the largest dipole moment.

Since the researched isosteres' NLO characteristics could not be experimentally verified, urea is employed in this work as a reference NLO molecule. Our results show that the more precise electron correlated methods MP2 yield more exact estimations of NLO for parent (Pyrrole) [65]. Our structures have a higher first hyperpolarizability than urea, which has a first hyperpolarizability of 0.1947 × 10-30 esu and dipole moment 1.373 Debye [61]. The most stable isostere, PN3B2, has a total dipole moment (0.812D) that is around two times lower than urea's (1.373 D) and has a first order hyperpolarizability (0.452 × 10-30 esu) that is roughly higher than urea's (0.1947 × 10-30 esu). This finding suggests that the researched structures are also appropriate for nonlinear optical uses. Table 5 shows that the values of first hyperpolarizability for PN3B2- PN3B4, PN3,4B2,5 - PN2, B3 -PN2, B4, and PN2,5B3,4 are 2, 5, 5, 11, 12, and 16 times greater than (urea), respectively.

Due to a longer - conjugated length and/or higher donor and acceptor capacity, the value typically increases along with the bathochromic effect. For example, the compounds PN3B4, PN2B4, and PN2,5B3,4 have bathochromic shift (see Tables 2, 5 and Fig. 4). Additionally, the lowest value of DR for (PN3B2, PN3B4, and PN2,5B3,4) and the highest value of HRS for (PN3,4B2,5) confirm short bond length, indicating greater selectivity. As a result, the studied isosteres have interesting optical characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of charges and the dipole moments
ce:section.ce:para: The dipole moments evaluated here by the two approaches: B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) and MP2/6-311G (2df,2p). The obtained values increase when passing from the gas phase to solvent, see Table 7 . The investigated isosteres are so sensitive to the solvent polarity, which has the order: water (H2O) > DMSO > CHCl3. The most polar species is PN2B4, which results in a large asymmetry in the atomic charge distribution and a high polarity of the NH and B-H bonds. While compound PN3B2 is the least polar, it has a more even distribution of partial atomic charges, Table 8 . It was noted that, the atomic charges are broadly improved in the solvation, and this leads consequently to such improvement in the molecule’s polarity.

Except for the two most stable isosteres, PN3B2 and PN3,4B2,5, the dipole moment of NB-pyrrole isosteres is around 2.4 times that of the pyrrole, (See also Table 7). The dipole moment vectors are affected by the polarity of the N-B bond. The vectorial sum of the distinct B-N dipole vectors, for example, can be used to calculate the electric dipole of N-B-pyrrole (Fig. 1). The net result of this vectorial sum is roughly equivalent to a single B-N dipole vector. It should be emphasized that the dipole moment vector points essentially through the direction of positive charges.

The values of atomic charges calculated using the NBO approach are presented in Table 8. Nonetheless, the NBO technique shows that boron atoms are the most positively charged, indicating that they are the most vulnerable to nucleophile assault. However, nitrogen atoms are the most negatively charged, indicating electrophilic attack sites.

Three solvents with varying polarity have been selected, including chloroform (CHCl3), a non-polar solvent with a low dielectric constant (ε = 4.8), dimethylsulfoxide (C2H6OS, DMSO), a polar solvent with a medium dielectric constant (ε = 46.7), and water (H2O), a polar solvent with a high dielectric constant (ε = 80) while, the gas phase is taken as a reference with a dielectric constant (ε = 1) to estimate the partial molar volumes of NB pyrrole isosteres. The findings show that, as the polarity of the isosteres increases, the molar volume of the considered systems decreases. This occurs explicitly in the highest polar water solvent (high dielectric constant (ε = 80)). However, in a nonpolar solvent, for instance, the situation is reversed and the molar volume increases consequently, see Table 7. This can be explained as a result of the inducing attractive and/or repulsive forces between the considered systems and polar solvents, which lead to a shrinkage of the molar volume of a polar system in a polar solvent comparing to gas phase or nonpolar solvent.

The present study concerns with the investigation of FTIR and FT-Raman of NB-pyrrole substituted groups, the vibrational frequencies at B3LYP/6-311++G (d,p) and MP2/6-311G (2df,2p) have been reported. Here, the non-linear isosteric structure of NB-substituted pyrrole with 10 atoms results in 24 normal modes of vibration (3 N-6, N = 10). The absorption spectra spam the range of 4000–400 cm−1. There are nine stretching, eight bending, and seven torsions modes in each group. Only the mono substituted group has CH vibrational modes.

From inspection of UV analysis of the considered compounds, the energy gap of the mono-substituted group is found to be lower than that of the double-substituted group. The most stable PN3B2 and PN3,4B2,5 isosteres have the highest excitation energy and blueshift wavelength comparing to the parent molecule. Take into consideration of solvation effect, the UV observed bands undergo a redshift (higher wavelength and lower excitation energy) with respect to the gas phase. The calculated λmax values increase from gas phase, then chloroform to DMSO showing bathochromic shift in all isosteres except PN3B2, PN3B4 and PN2,5B3,4 isosteres.

According to NMR calculations, the NH1 proton was assigned at the largest chemical shift (8.51 ppm) for the PN3B4 isostere. The PN2B4 isostere's BH1 proton signal exhibits the smallest chemical shift of 4.47 ppm. According to the NLO study, the PN2B4 has the most polarizability and dipole moment. Actually, the values of dipole moment increase passing from the gas phase to the different considered solvents: CHCl3, DMSO, and eventually H2O. This can be attributed to the increasing of atomic charges as a function of increasing the solvents’ polarity.
